HALVERTON — The first passengers boarded the Halverton harbour tram on Saturday morning, riding the pale green carriages along the newly laid waterfront line as gulls wheeled overhead and a brass band played on the quay.

The three-kilometre route, a decade in the making, runs from the old fish market at the western end of the harbour to the ferry terminal in the east, tracing the curve of the seawall the whole way. City planners have called it the centrepiece of a long effort to reconnect Halverton's residents with a waterfront that decades of warehouses and car parks had walled off.

A crowd gathered at the fish market stop well before the seven o'clock launch. Children pressed against the barriers to watch the lead tram glide in, its bell ringing, and a cheer went up as the doors folded open and the mayor stepped aside to let a retired dockworker take the first seat.

"I loaded ships on this quay for thirty-one years," said Tomas Vale, ninety, who cut the ribbon at the market stop. "Now I can ride the length of it in a padded chair and watch the boats come in. I never thought I would see it."

The line's twelve carriages were built at a workshop in the northern industrial quarter and painted the same soft green as the harbour pilot boats, a colour chosen after a public vote. Each tram carries up to ninety passengers and runs on power drawn from overhead wires strung on slim grey poles designed, planners said, to stay low enough not to block the view of the water.

Not everyone welcomed the project. Some harbourside business owners complained that two years of construction had driven away customers, and a group of them gathered near the ferry terminal on Saturday holding signs asking the city to lower their rents. City officials acknowledged the disruption but pointed to studies predicting that foot traffic along the waterfront would double within three years.

For the opening weekend, rides were free. By mid-morning the trams were running full, and the queue at the fish market stop stretched back past the oyster stalls. Passengers leaned out of the windows to photograph the fishing fleet, the lighthouse at the harbour mouth, and the long white wake of the departing ferry.

Service will settle into a regular schedule from Monday, with trams every eight minutes through the day. Planners say a second line, climbing from the harbour into the hillside neighbourhoods above, is already on the drawing board.
